# Agent Process Guide — {project_name}

**Generated by AI-GCE** | Last updated: {date}

This guide explains WHEN to call each governance agent, WHAT happens if you don't, and HOW to recover from missed invocations. Agents are your process checkpoints — hooks handle real-time code enforcement automatically, agents handle governance milestones deliberately.

---

## Quick Reference

| Shortcut | Agent | When to Call | What Breaks If You Don't |
|----------|-------|-------------|--------------------------|
| `SDC__` | Session Discipline | Start of every AI session | Vibe coding, no spec-before-code |
| `SGV__` | Sprint Governance | Sprint boundary (start/end) | Sprint goals untracked, retro actions lost |
| `CRV__` | Code Review | Before approving any PR | Review quality unverified, role isolation bypassed |
| `SQC__` | Steering Quality | After editing any steering file | Stale/contradictory steering, wrong hook patterns |
| `CMG__` | Change Management | Before any release/deployment | Untested changes reach production |
| `DOD__` | Definition of Done | Before marking any task "complete" | Incomplete work passes as done |

**How to call:** Type the shortcut (e.g., `SDC__`) anywhere in your chat prompt. The agent activates immediately.

---

## The Three-Layer Enforcement Model

Your workspace has three complementary enforcement layers:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ LAYER 1: HOOKS (automatic, real-time)                                │
│ ─────────────────────────────────────                                │
│ Fire on fileEdited or agentStop. Catch code violations as they       │
│ happen. You never call these — they run silently in the background.  │
│ Silent when passing. Warn only when something is wrong.              │
│                                                                      │
│ Examples: sensitive-data-check, security-gate, migration-safety,     │
│           naming-check, module-boundary, coverage-check              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ LAYER 2: PROCESS AGENTS (manual, milestone-based) ← THIS GUIDE      │
│ ─────────────────────────────────────────────────                    │
│ You invoke these at specific workflow milestones. They validate       │
│ governance practices that require human judgment to trigger.          │
│ They can't fire automatically because only YOU know when a sprint    │
│ boundary, a review, or a release is happening.                       │
│                                                                      │
│ Examples: session-discipline, sprint-governance, code-review,        │
│           steering-quality, change-management, DoD-gate              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ LAYER 3: AUDIT AGENT (periodic, comprehensive)                       │
│ ─────────────────────────────────────────────────                    │
│ Full codebase compliance scan. Produces a score, identifies drift,   │
│ and catches what Layers 1 and 2 missed. Your safety net.             │
│ Run monthly, or when audit score is needed for stakeholders.         │
│                                                                      │
│ Invocation: "Run a full compliance audit"                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Hooks protect you automatically. Agents protect you deliberately. The audit catches everything else.

---

## Process Cadence Map

### 🔄 Every AI Session (Daily or More)

**Call:** `SDC__` (Session Discipline)

| What It Checks | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Spec-before-code discipline | Prevents undocumented implementation |
| Session sizing and scope | Prevents scope creep within a session |
| Correction escalation protocol | Catches compounding errors early |
| Context front-loading | Ensures relevant files are loaded before work starts |

**When exactly:** Before writing any implementation code. After opening the workspace. As your first action in a new coding session.

---

### 🏃 Every Sprint Boundary (Weekly/Bi-weekly)

**Call:** `SGV__` (Sprint Governance)

| What It Checks | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Sprint plan completeness | Ensures goals are defined, not just task lists |
| Capacity allocation | Prevents overcommitment |
| Previous retro action follow-through | Ensures continuous improvement actually happens |
| Velocity tracking | Enables forecasting and stakeholder visibility |

**When exactly:** At sprint planning (validates the plan) AND at sprint review/retro (validates completeness).

---

### 🔍 Every Code Review

**Call:** `CRV__` (Code Review)

| What It Checks | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Reviewer ≠ Author | Segregation of duties (audit requirement) |
| Trust spectrum compliance | Right expertise reviews right code |
| Per-layer review focus | Reviewer checks what matters for that layer |
| Review evidence | Proves review happened (audit trail) |

**When exactly:** After finishing a review, before clicking "Approve" or "Merge."

---

### 📝 Every Steering Change

**Call:** `SQC__` (Steering Quality)

| What It Checks | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Context budget (≤300 lines) | Prevents Kiro from silently dropping files |
| Cross-file contradictions | Prevents non-deterministic AI behavior |
| Prescriptive language quality | Ensures rules are enforceable (MUST/NEVER) |
| Freshness against codebase | Prevents hooks firing on stale paths |

**When exactly:** After saving any edit to a `.kiro/steering/*.md` file. Also: monthly health check.

**Self-healing:** This agent checks current state — calling it late still catches all current issues.

---

### 🚀 Every Release/Deployment

**Call:** `CMG__` (Change Management)

| What It Checks | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Change plan exists | Governs what's changing and why |
| UAT traceability | Proves features were tested |
| Stakeholder sign-off | Authorizes the release |
| Rollback criteria + procedure | Prepares for failure scenarios |
| Communication plan | No surprises for operations/support |

**When exactly:** After final testing, BEFORE production deployment. This is your last gate.

**⚠️ CRITICAL:** This is the most consequential agent to skip. Production incidents from ungoverned changes are expensive and visible.

---

### ✅ Every Task Completion

**Call:** `DOD__` (Definition of Done)

| What It Checks | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Acceptance criteria met | Feature actually satisfies the requirement |
| Test evidence exists | Coverage gaps don't slip through |
| Documentation updated | Knowledge stays current |
| No unresolved TODOs | Hidden debt doesn't accumulate |

**When exactly:** After implementation is done and tests pass, before moving the ticket to "Done."

---

## What Happens If You Skip

### Consequence Severity Matrix

| Agent Skipped | Severity | Detection Delay | Self-Healing? |
|---------------|----------|-----------------|---------------|
| `SDC__` Session Discipline | 🟡 Medium | Next audit | ❌ Must retroactively write specs |
| `SGV__` Sprint Governance | 🟡 Medium | Next sprint/audit | ⚠️ Partial — can't reconstruct past sprint |
| `CRV__` Code Review | 🟠 High | Audit (git history) | ❌ Cannot undo merges |
| `SQC__` Steering Quality | 🟢 Low | Next invocation | ✅ Fully self-healing |
| `CMG__` Change Management | 🔴 Critical | Post-deployment | ❌ Cannot undo production deployment |
| `DOD__` Definition of Done | 🟡 Medium | Audit | ⚠️ Re-open + fix gaps |

### Escalation Model

When you repeatedly skip calling a process agent at its designated milestone, the audit agent detects the gap and escalates:

| Missed Invocations | What Happens | What You See |
|:------------------:|--------------|--------------|
| **1–2 times** | Audit score dips slightly. No alarm. | Dashboard score drops a few percent — normal for early adoption. |
| **3–5 times** | Audit flags "process adherence declining." | 🟡 Warning on dashboard: *"{Agent} not invoked for N consecutive milestones."* |
| **6+ times** | Formal remediation entry created. | Remediation ticket: *"Action required — acknowledge gap and resume cadence or waive via exception."* |

### Recovery Procedures

| If You Missed... | Do This |
|------------------|---------|
| `SDC__` for 3+ sessions | Run audit → find unspecified code → write retroactive specs |
| `SGV__` for a sprint | Run `SGV__` now → reconstruct from git history → update registers |
| `CRV__` before merge | Log exception → post-merge review → update branch protection |
| `SQC__` after editing | Run `SQC__` now → it checks current state → self-healing |
| `CMG__` before deploy | **If still pre-prod:** STOP, run `CMG__`, fix, then deploy. **If in prod:** Exception + post-mortem. |
| `DOD__` before "done" | Re-open task → run `DOD__` → fix gaps → re-close |

---

## Relationship to Hooks

| What | Hooks Handle (Automatic) | Agents Handle (Manual) |
|------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| Code quality | ✅ Security, naming, boundaries, coverage | — |
| Session quality | ✅ Module boundaries on agentStop | `SDC__` validates methodology |
| Process quality | — | ✅ Sprints, reviews, DoD, releases |
| System quality | — | ✅ Full audit (periodic) |

**The rule:** If a check must fire every time (code save, session end) → it's a hook. If a check fires at a human-decided milestone (sprint end, before deploy) → it's an agent.

---

## Tier Activation

Not all agents are active from Day 0. They activate progressively:

| Tier | Active Agents | When Available |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| **Tier 1** (Day 0) | `SDC__` Session Discipline | From first AI-GCE generation |
| **Tier 2** (Sprint 2+) | + `SGV__`, `CRV__`, `SQC__`, `DOD__` | After Tier 2 activation |
| **Tier 3** (Pre-Release) | + `CMG__` Change Management | After Tier 3 activation |

Check your current tier: read `.compliance-state.json` → `"complianceTier"` field.

To activate the next tier: say "Activate next compliance tier" — AI-GCE handles it.

---

## Full Agent Registry

See `.governance/AGENT_REGISTRY.md` for the complete list of all agents (including audit and infrastructure agents not covered in this process guide).
